title: Cat Pain When Touching Lower Back or Belly: Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn why your cat shows pain when touched on the back or belly, the urgency of these symptoms, and how to help your vet triage the issue.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sensitivity to touch in the lower back or belly indicates your cat is in pain and requires a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours, though immediate emergency care is needed if they cannot urinate or are vomiting persistently.

Why is my cat sensitive to being touched on their lower back or belly?

  • Signs of physical distress include reacting with sensitivity, hissing, or recoiling when touched.
  • Sensitivity in the lower back may indicate feline hyperesthesia, spinal arthritis, or muscular strain.
  • Sensitivity in the belly or abdomen often points toward internal issues like gastrointestinal upset, constipation, urinary tract obstructions, or organ inflammation.

How urgent is it if my cat shows discomfort in their back or abdomen?

  • Medium Urgency: This situation requires a veterinary examination within 24 to 48 hours to address your cat's pain.
  • Immediate Emergency: Seek emergency care right away if your cat is unable to urinate, is vomiting persistently, or has pale gums, as these are signs of a critical crisis.

How can photos and videos help my vet diagnose my cat's discomfort?

  • A video showing your cat’s gait or their reaction to being touched provides clinical clues that descriptions alone might miss.
  • Footage of a 'tucked up' sitting posture can help identify the severity of the pain.
  • Photos of the specific area can help identify visible swelling, skin lesions, or bruising contributing to the discomfort.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats presenting with pain upon touching the lower back or belly, acute abdominal pain requires localization. Examination should include auscultation of the abdomen for bowel sounds to localize the problem to the reticuloendothelial, reproductive, urinary, or GI systems; the peritoneal space; or the muscle, skin, nerves, or fat around the abdominal wall. An initial minimum database should consist of a PCV, total solids, glucose, and BUN. Other important diagnostics include urinalysis (before fluid administration), venous or arterial blood gas, an electrolyte panel, a CBC, and a serum chemistry panel. When coagulation disorders are suspected or surgery is anticipated, blood smears to estimate platelet number and a clotting profile should be performed.

Chapter: Urology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1532)

faqs.question: What does it mean if my cat's skin ripples when I touch their back?
faqs.answer: Skin rippling or twitching can be a sign of Feline Hyperesthesia Syndrome, a condition where the cat's skin and nerves are hypersensitive to touch.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat Aspirin or Tylenol for the pain?
faqs.answer: No. Never give human pain medication to a cat. These drugs are highly toxic to felines and can lead to fatal liver failure or kidney damage.

faqs.question: Is a firm belly a sign of a problem?
faqs.answer: Yes, a tense or 'board-like' abdomen often indicates significant internal pain or inflammation and should be evaluated by a vet as soon as possible.
